4:30 PM – Teaching a Graduate Seminar: “The Sacred Zoo: Animals as Divine Vessels”  
In the university’s art history seminar room, he leads a discussion with six graduate students on the metaphysical role of animals in Egyptian cosmology. He brings in three 3D-printed replicas of animal statues (a lion, a crocodile, and a falcon) to demonstrate how scale, material (granite vs. bronze), and placement in temple precincts dictated their ritual function. He asks students to analyze a fragment of a faience amulet showing a cat swallowing a serpent—prompting them to consider whether this is a protective symbol, a mythic narrative, or a metaphor for ma’at overcoming isfet. The session ends with students debating whether ancient Egyptians viewed animals as divine embodiments or mere conduits.
